danger_signals.visual_form: Character falling to ground, count mechanic (3 knockdowns = elimination)
danger_signals.meaning: Vulnerability state; cumulative danger in Grudge Matches

danger_signals.visual_form: Final boss character with distinctive masked appearance
danger_signals.meaning: Ultimate threat; final challenge after 15 standard matches

danger_signals.visual_form: NPCs attacking, dropping weapons, or pushing players
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental hazard; unpredictable danger from arena inhabitants

safety_signals.meaning: Temporary invulnerability/damage reduction; brief safety window
Progression secured; bonus monetary reward gained

safety_signals.visual_form: Match counter (1-15 standard, special Grudge/Championship matches)
safety_signals.meaning: Distance toward goal; structural safety in tournament format

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Buzz (wrestler), Ty (kickboxer), Kato (martial artist) — visually distinct by body type and fighting style
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Arena fighter status; digitized live-action portrayal distinguishes them from opponents

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Character archetypes: Executioner, Southside Jim, Angel, C.C. Rider, Mad Miles, Heavy Metal, Chainman Eddie
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Each opponent has unique visual identity and weapon association (Knife Woman, Knife Man carry blades)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Progressive opponent roster; thematic naming and appearance signal underground fighting pit culture

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Solo final opponent; serves as antagonistic taunt-giver between matches
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Tournament apex; positioned as shadowy orchestrator of pit-fighting events

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Neutral-to-hostile NPCs; can become aggressive actors in match space
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Visual audience presence; weapons and interference suggest lawless underground arena setting

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Standard opponent roster; single-fighter matches with 15 sequential fights
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: CPU-controlled clone appears every third fight (Grudge Match); single-player progression structure

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Up to 3 simultaneous players on-screen with CPU-controlled additional opponents
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Grudge Match rules: players fight each other; 3 knockdowns = elimination; last player standing advances

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Masked Warrior appears; final match screen
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: If multiplayer, remaining players must eliminate each other before facing Masked Warrior; final boss battle

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: NPCs visible in arena edges; weapons drop into play space; pushing/attacking animations
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Crowd can disrupt match flow; players pushed back into combat; environmental hazards deployed

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Power pill object appears on arena floor
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Temporary stat boost; player damage output increases, incoming damage reduces

environmental_storytelling.example: Arena populated by civilian crowd and unruly NPCs; weapons available on ground; loose social contract of brutal combat suggests illegal/underground operation
Opponents named 'Executioner,' 'Chainman Eddie,' 'Knife Woman' convey criminal/violent subculture; character backgrounds (wrestler, kickboxer, martial artist) establish skill hierarchy
Real human performers captured via bluescreen create immersive presence; bodies and clothing variations signal diverse socioeconomic origins and fighting traditions
15-match sequence implies survival tournament; increasing opponent strength signals escalating stakes toward final confrontation with Masked Warrior
Masked Warrior taunts between matches; positioned as orchestrator/overseer; mask and singular appearance suggest supernatural or hidden-identity threat
Audience interference (weapon drops, pushing, attacks) frames arena as chaotic, lawless space; removes referee authority and suggests mob mentality

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Wrestler-style moves; high strength, lower agility; grapple-oriented attacks
Kickboxer moves; balanced speed/power; kick-oriented attack set
Martial artist moves; quick strikes, black belt techniques; high speed, lower raw power
CPU-controlled fighters attack until energy depleted; Grudge Match clones mirror player behavior; varied fighting styles per opponent archetype
Issues periodic taunts; narrative presence without direct interaction
Final boss combat; unique move set; positioned as ultimate opponent requiring victory for tournament completion
Attack players, drop weapons, push combatants; can interfere with match flow; suggests mob mentality and chaotic arena culture
Simultaneous three-button press triggers enhanced attack; temporary power spike

notes: Pit-Fighter uses digitized live-action actors (1990 arcade original), creating visual authenticity rare for early fighting games. SNES version lacks interactive audience and three characters (Southside Jim, Heavy Metal, Mad Miles), reducing environmental storytelling capacity. Game communicates world-state primarily through match structure (15-count tournament, Grudge Matches every third fight, final Masked Warrior confrontation) and crowd behavior. Danger signals rely on health depletion mechanics and knockdown counting. Factions are minimal — distinction between player-selected fighter, CPU opponents, and final boss. No explicit political/faction conflict; world-state is primarily tournament progression. Unused graphics ('TIME UP,' 'ELIMINATION') suggest features planned but removed, indicating design iteration.
